title: Titanic
role_tag_text: plays the jealous fiancé Cal Hockley
explain: James Cameron literally drew the portrait of Kate Winslet's character — the 'draw me like one of your French girls' sketch.

title: The Dark Knight
role_tag_text: plays the district attorney Harvey Dent who becomes Two-Face
explain: Heath Ledger's posthumous Oscar win made him only the second actor to win an Academy Award after death.

title: Gladiator
role_tag_text: plays Marcus Aurelius's daughter Lucilla
explain: Russell Crowe improvised the line 'Are you not entertained?' during filming — it became one of cinema's most quoted moments.

title: Home Alone
role_tag_text: plays Marv Merchants, the taller and clumsier of the two burglars
explain: The film spent 12 weeks at number one in the US — the highest-grossing live-action comedy of all time until 2011.

title: Shrek
role_tag_text: voices Princess Fiona
explain: Mike Myers re-recorded all his lines with a Scottish accent after initially recording them in his natural Canadian voice.

title: The Princess Bride
role_tag_text: plays Inigo Montoya, the swordsman seeking to avenge his father
explain: The studio wanted to change Inigo's famous line — Rob Reiner fought to keep it. Now it's one of the most quoted lines in film history.

title: The Social Network
role_tag_text: plays co-founder Eduardo Saverin
explain: David Fincher had the cast rehearse for months and required up to 99 takes per scene — Andrew Garfield memorized a 12-minute monologue.

title: No Country for Old Men
role_tag_text: plays Llewelyn Moss, the welder who stumbles upon drug money
explain: Javier Bardem's Anton Chigurh won him the Oscar and was voted the most terrifying villain in cinema history in multiple polls.

title: Pretty Woman
role_tag_text: plays Philip Stuckey, the slimy lawyer
explain: The original script was a dark drama called $3000 — the transformation into a rom-com happened during production.

title: Mean Girls
role_tag_text: plays the writer and English teacher Ms. Norbury
explain: Tina Fey wrote the screenplay based on Rosalind Wiseman's self-help book Queen Bees and Wannabes — October 3rd is now an unofficial holiday.

title: Heat
role_tag_text: plays the marksman Chris Shiherlis
explain: Al Pacino and Robert De Niro had never actually shared a scene before the famous diner confrontation — it was their first time on screen together.

title: Bridesmaids
role_tag_text: plays the outrageous and scene-stealing Megan
explain: Melissa McCarthy's Oscar nomination for Best Supporting Actress was the first for a comedic performance in the category in decades.

title: Interstellar
role_tag_text: plays the adult version of Murph, the daughter left behind on Earth
explain: Physicist Kip Thorne's equations for the film led to two scientific papers — the visual rendering of the black hole Gargantua was scientifically accurate.

title: The Departed
role_tag_text: plays mob boss Frank Costello
explain: Scorsese's remake of the Hong Kong film Infernal Affairs finally won him the long-awaited Oscar for Best Director.

title: Mamma Mia!
role_tag_text: plays Sam Carmichael, one of three possible fathers
choices: Mamma Mia! Here We Go Again
explain: Pierce Brosnan openly admitted he knew his singing was weak — it actually made his character more endearing to audiences.

title: Knives Out
role_tag_text: plays Marta Cabrera, the nurse at the center of the mystery
explain: Chris Evans was cast against type as the villainous Hugh Ransom — the role reinvigorated his career after playing Captain America.

title: The Grand Budapest Hotel
role_tag_text: plays Agatha, the lobby boy's love interest and pastry expert
explain: Wes Anderson shot the film in three different aspect ratios to visually represent each time period in the story.

title: Bohemian Rhapsody
role_tag_text: plays Brian May, Queen's legendary guitarist
explain: Rami Malek wore prosthetic teeth to replicate Freddie Mercury's prominent overjet and trained with a vocal coach for months.

title: Black Panther
role_tag_text: plays Nakia, the Wakandan spy and T'Challa's love interest
explain: The first superhero film nominated for Best Picture at the Oscars and the highest-grossing film by a Black director at time of release.

title: Catch Me If You Can
role_tag_text: plays the con man's father, Frank Sr.
explain: Frank Abagnale Jr. — the real con man — later served as a consultant on the film and has claimed a career more fabulous than the movie itself.

title: The Lion King
role_tag_text: voices Mufasa, the king and Simba's father
explain: James Earl Jones and Madge Sinclair reprised their roles from Coming to America (1988) — they played an African king and queen in that film too.

title: Avengers: Endgame
role_tag_text: plays Black Widow, who makes the ultimate sacrifice on Vormir
explain: Highest-grossing film of all time for several years, beating Titanic and Avatar before a re-release was used to surpass Avatar.

title: A Beautiful Mind
role_tag_text: plays William Parcher, the mysterious government agent
explain: Russell Crowe learned to write complex mathematical equations on screen left-handed after studying footage of the real John Nash.

title: Crazy Rich Asians
role_tag_text: plays Eleanor Young, the formidable mother-in-law
choices: To All the Boys I've Loved Before
explain: The first Hollywood film with an all-Asian lead cast since The Joy Luck Club in 1993 — 25 years later.

title: The Breakfast Club
role_tag_text: plays John Bender, the rebellious outsider with a troubled home life
explain: John Hughes wrote the screenplay in just three days — and the cast largely improvised the famous group therapy scene in the library.
